1: 'And I lifted my eyes and saw, and behold, a man with a measuring line in his hand!' The third night vision: a surveyor with a measuring line (chevel middah). The measuring line evokes the rebuilding of Jerusalem — a surveyor's first task is to measure the site before construction. In the context of the night visions, the surveyor is divine or angelic, marking out YHWH's intention for the restored city. The measuring line appears in Ezekiel's temple vision (Ezekiel 40:3-5) as the instrument for measuring the eschatological temple complex.
2: 'Then I said, Where are you going? And he said to me, To measure Jerusalem, to see what is its width and what is its length.' The surveyor's mission: the dimensions of restored Jerusalem. This is not demolition measuring but rebuilding planning. The question of Jerusalem's dimensions in the vision context becomes the vehicle for the theological declaration that follows: Jerusalem will be too full for walls (Zechariah 2:4).
3: 'And behold, the angel who talked with me came forward, and another angel came forward to meet him.' The movement of angels in the visionary space — the interpreting angel and another angel engage in dialogue, which Zechariah overhears. The angelic conversation model (as in Job 1-2; Daniel 10:13) presents the divine council's discussion as accessible to the prophet through angelic mediation.
4: 'And said to him, Run, say to that young man, Jerusalem shall be inhabited as villages without walls, because of the multitude of people and livestock in it.' The radical declaration: Jerusalem will be wall-less — not because it has been destroyed but because it will be too full for walls to contain it. This directly contradicts the priority Nehemiah would later place on wall-building (Neh 1-6). In the eschatological vision, walls are a mark of limitation, not security. The unlimited expansion of population and livestock signals covenant-blessing abundance (cf. Deuteronomy 28:4-5, 11).
5: 'And I will be to her a wall of fire all around, declares YHWH, and I will be the glory in her midst.' The replacement for city walls: YHWH himself as a wall of fire (choma esh) — the theophanic fire that terrifies enemies (Exodus 13:21-22; Zechariah 14:24; Isaiah 4:5). The pillar of fire that protected Israel in the desert now becomes the permanent boundary of restored Jerusalem. 'I will be the glory in her midst' (lekavod ehyeh betocha) — the Shekinah glory that departed before the exile (Ezekiel 10:18-19; Zechariah 11:22-23) returns to the center of the city.
6: 'Up! Up! Flee from the land of the north, declares YHWH. For I have spread you abroad as the four winds of the heavens, declares YHWH.' The urgent summons to the diaspora: flee Babylon (the north, the direction from which Babylon's armies came). The four-winds scattering of Israel (the diaspora across all compass points) is now reversed — YHWH calls his people home. The same four-winds imagery in Ezekiel 37:9 (the valley of dry bones) and Jeremiah 49:36.
7: 'Up! Escape to Zion, you who dwell with the daughter of Babylon.' The specific address to those in Babylon: escape now, while the escape is possible. The urgency echoes Jeremiah 51:6 ('Flee from the midst of Babylon; let every one save his life!'). The choice of whether to return to Zion or remain comfortable in Babylon was a real and difficult one for the exiled community — many had prospered in Babylon and had no desire to undertake the arduous journey to a ruined land.
8: 'For thus said YHWH of hosts, after his glory sent me to the nations who plundered you, for he who touches you touches the apple of his eye.' The famous phrase: the apple of YHWH's eye (bebavat aino — literally 'the pupil of his eye'). To touch Israel is to poke YHWH in the pupil — the most sensitive and reflexively protected part of the body. The image expresses the extreme tenderness and protectiveness of YHWH's relationship to his people. Deuteronomy 32:10 uses the same image of YHWH guarding Israel in the wilderness.
9: 'For behold, I will shake my hand over them, and they shall become plunder for those who served them. Then you will know that YHWH of hosts has sent me.' The plunderers become the plundered — the reversal of fortune that accompanies the restoration. The oracle ends with the messenger-formula: 'you will know that YHWH of hosts has sent me' — the authentication of the prophetic word is its fulfillment in history.
10: 'Sing and rejoice, O daughter of Zion, for behold, I come and I will dwell in your midst, declares YHWH.' The restoration hymn: sing (roni), rejoice (vesimchi), for YHWH comes to dwell in her midst. The divine indwelling (shachanti) — the Shekinah theology that the temple mediates. The daughter of Zion is addressed as the feminine personification of the covenant community assembled in Jerusalem, waiting for the divine bridegroom's arrival.
11: 'And many nations shall join themselves to YHWH in that day, and shall be my people. And I will dwell in your midst, and you shall know that YHWH of hosts has sent me to you.' The eschatological inclusion of the nations: many Gentile peoples will join themselves to YHWH and be called 'my people' (le'am) — the covenant term previously reserved for Israel. The mission to the nations, implicit in Zephaniah 3:9 and Joel 2:32, here becomes explicit: YHWH's dwelling in Zion will attract the nations to covenant relationship.
12: 'And YHWH will inherit Judah as his portion in the holy land, and will again choose Jerusalem.' Judah as YHWH's inheritance (nachalah) — the reversal of the language of divine election. Usually Israel is said to inherit the land; here YHWH inherits Judah. The land and the people are YHWH's possession, his portion among the nations. The re-choice of Jerusalem (bachar od beYerushalaim) repeats Zechariah 1:17 — the election stands and is renewed.
13: 'Be silent, all flesh, before YHWH, for he has roused himself from his holy dwelling.' The summons to cosmic silence (has) before YHWH — the same summons as Habakkuk 2:20 and Zephaniah 1:7. YHWH has aroused himself (ne'or) from the heavenly dwelling to act in history. The divine awakening from apparent inactivity (cf. Psalms 44:23 'Awake! Why are you sleeping, O Lord?') signals that the long period of divine restraint is ending and the active phase of restoration has begun.
